title: PayCore вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Financial Transactions & Ledger System
stack: Go В· PostgreSQL В· Redis В· Kafka В· gRPC В· Next.js

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
PayCore is a production-grade financial transaction and ledger system built in Go. It demonstrates the hardest correctness problems in backend engineering: double-entry bookkeeping, idempotency across retries, event sourcing, and distributed Saga-based payment flows. Every mutation is guaranteed to be safe to retry; every ledger entry is balanced; every payment failure triggers a compensating transaction.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Double-entry bookkeeping вҖ” every debit has a matching credit, ledger always balances
- Idempotency keys вҖ” every mutation safe to retry with no duplicate charges
- Saga pattern вҖ” distributed payment flow with compensating transactions on failure
- Event sourcing вҖ” account state reconstructed from event replay
- JWT RS256 auth + refresh token rotation
- HMAC-signed webhooks for payment callbacks

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Technology | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Auth Service | Go + JWT RS256 | Login, token issuance, refresh rotation, Redis revocation |
| Ledger Service | Go + PostgreSQL | Double-entry bookkeeping, account balances, audit trail |
| Payment Orchestrator | Go + Kafka + Saga | Multi-step payment flow, compensating transactions |
| Event Store | PostgreSQL + Kafka | Immutable event log, state reconstruction by replay |
| Webhook Delivery | Go + HMAC | Signed outbound notifications to merchant callbacks |
| Frontend | Next.js + Tanstack Query | Transaction dashboard, ledger viewer, payment flow UI |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Double-Entry Bookkeeping

Every financial movement creates two ledger entries: a debit on the source account and an equal credit on the destination. The ledger INSERT is wrapped in a transaction with a CHECK constraint: `SUM(debits) = SUM(credits)`. Any imbalance causes a constraint violation and rolls back. This eliminates an entire class of accounting bugs that affect most fintech systems.

### Idempotency Key Pattern

Every payment mutation accepts an `idempotency_key` header. The key is stored with the result in a Redis hash (TTL: 24 hours). On retry, if the key exists, the stored result is returned immediately вҖ” no second charge, no duplicate ledger entry. The key covers the full response, not just the status code.

### Saga Pattern вҖ” Choreography

A payment flow spans three services: reserve funds, charge payment gateway, confirm ledger. Each step publishes a Kafka event. If the gateway charge fails, a compensating event releases the reserved funds. The Kafka outbox pattern guarantees events are published even if the producer crashes between database write and Kafka publish.

### Event Sourcing

Account state is never stored directly. The events table is the source of truth: `AccountOpened`, `FundsDeposited`, `PaymentInitiated`, `PaymentCompleted`, `PaymentFailed`. Current balance is computed by replaying events. Snapshots are taken every 1000 events to avoid full replay on startup.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
- `accounts (id, owner_id, currency, created_at)`
- `ledger_entries (id, account_id, amount, direction, transaction_id, created_at)`
- `payment_events (id, aggregate_id, type, payload JSONB, sequence, created_at)`
- `idempotency_keys (key, result JSONB, created_at)` вҖ” Redis, TTL 24h
- `outbox (id, topic, payload, published_at)` вҖ” transactional Kafka guarantee

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Payment throughput | 5,000 TPS (k6, 50 concurrent users) |
| Ledger INSERT p99 | < 8ms |
| Saga compensation time | < 200ms end-to-end |
| Event replay (10K events) | < 50ms |
| Idempotency lookup (Redis) | < 1ms |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes вҖ” concurrent ledger access must be safe
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on every query вҖ” zero seq scans on tables > 10K rows
- Idempotency key on every mutation that can be retried
- Outbox pattern for every Kafka publish that must be guaranteed
- Saga compensating transactions triggered and verified on payment failure
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` passes вҖ” goroutine leaks in payment workers accumulate

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Infraspec Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| 3вҖ“5 years or equivalent | Production-deployed with k6 benchmarks and ADRs |
| Double-entry accounting | Full ledger with constraint-enforced balance |
| Distributed transactions | Saga choreography with compensating events |
| Event-driven architecture | Kafka outbox, event sourcing, CQRS read model |
| Security best practices | JWT RS256, HMAC webhooks, input sanitization |

title: RouteMaster вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Logistics & Notifications Platform
stack: Next.js В· Go В· Elasticsearch В· Kafka В· Redis В· PostgreSQL

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
RouteMaster is a logistics and real-time notifications platform built with Go and Next.js, demonstrating fan-out notifications at scale, Elasticsearch full-text search, a Bloom-filter-backed web crawler, and real-time order tracking via WebSocket. It integrates directly with the Notification Delivery Service (a standalone biweekly project) for multi-channel delivery and serves as the primary consumer of DungBeetle's job queue for async work.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Fan-out notifications at scale вҖ” 10,000 simultaneous delivery confirmations
- Elasticsearch full-text search with BM25 relevance scoring and faceted filters
- Bloom-filter-backed web crawler вҖ” avoids re-crawling seen URLs in O(1) space
- Real-time order tracking via WebSocket with Redis pub/sub fan-out
- Kafka-backed notification pipeline with at-least-once delivery guarantees
- Next.js Server Components with SSE for live shipment status updates

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Technology | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Order Service | Go + PostgreSQL | Order lifecycle, status transitions, driver assignment |
| Tracking Service | Go + WebSocket + Redis | Real-time location updates, fan-out to connected clients |
| Search Service | Go + Elasticsearch | Full-text shipment search, faceted filters, relevance |
| Crawler | Go + Bloom filter | Address/logistics data crawling, deduplication |
| Notification Pipeline | Kafka + Delivery Service | Fan-out dispatch, multi-channel delivery, receipts |
| Frontend | Next.js + SSE | Order tracker, live map, search UI, notification feed |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Fan-Out Notifications at Scale

When a shipment status changes, a Kafka event triggers the Notification Pipeline. The fan-out worker reads all subscribers for the shipment (sender, receiver, account manager) and dispatches to the Notification Delivery Service via its REST API. The Delivery Service handles multi-channel routing (email, SMS, push, webhook). The fan-out is parallelised with a Go `errgroup` вҖ” all 10,000 notifications are dispatched concurrently with a semaphore to prevent connection pool exhaustion.

### Elasticsearch Full-Text Search

Shipment search uses Elasticsearch with a BM25 relevance scorer. The index maps: `origin_address` (text, analyzed), `destination_address` (text, analyzed), `carrier_name` (keyword), `status` (keyword), `created_at` (date). Queries combine full-text match on addresses with term filters on status and date range. Faceted search returns aggregation buckets: by carrier, by status, by date. The ADR documents the decision to use Elasticsearch over PostgreSQL full-text search at this data volume.

### Bloom Filter Web Crawler

The logistics data crawler collects address and carrier information from public sources. A Bloom filter (false positive rate: 0.1%) tracks seen URLs in O(1) space вҖ” far more memory-efficient than a visited set. Before fetching a URL, the filter is checked; only URLs that pass (not seen) are fetched. The filter is persisted to Redis on shutdown and reloaded on startup. False positive rate is measured and documented in `BENCHMARKS.md`.

### Real-Time Order Tracking

Driver location updates flow: Driver app вҶ’ Go WebSocket server вҶ’ Redis pub/sub вҶ’ all connected tracking clients. Redis pub/sub enables horizontal scaling: any WebSocket server node can fan-out updates to its connected clients regardless of which node the driver is connected to. The WebSocket server uses `gorilla/websocket` with a ping/pong keepalive (30s interval). The presence registry uses Redis HSET with a 60s TTL.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
- `orders (id, sender_id, recipient_id, status, driver_id, created_at, updated_at)`
- `shipment_events (id, order_id, event_type, location POINT, metadata JSONB, created_at)`
- `notification_subscriptions (id, order_id, user_id, channels TEXT[], created_at)`
- `crawler_seeds (id, url, domain, crawled_at, status)`
- Elasticsearch index: `shipments (origin, destination, carrier, status, created_at)`

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Fan-out throughput | 10,000 notifications dispatched in < 2s (errgroup + semaphore) |
| WebSocket connections | 5,000 concurrent tracking sessions, < 50ms delivery p99 |
| Elasticsearch search p99 | < 80ms (full-text + facet, 1M documents) |
| Crawler throughput | 500 URLs/sec, Bloom filter false positive rate: 0.08% |
| Order status update latency | < 100ms from driver app to tracking client |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes вҖ” concurrent WebSocket fan-out has race conditions
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` вҖ” goroutine leaks in tracking server accumulate over hours
- Fan-out semaphore tuned: connection pool exhaustion tested and prevented
- Bloom filter false positive rate measured and documented
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on every PostgreSQL query, Elasticsearch `explain` on every search
- WebSocket ping/pong tested: dead connections purged within 2 TTL windows

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Infraspec Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| NoSQL and Elasticsearch | Full-text search, BM25, faceted aggregations, hybrid search |
| Real-time systems | WebSocket, SSE, Redis pub/sub, distributed presence |
| Event-driven architecture | Kafka fan-out, outbox pattern, notification pipeline |
| 0-to-1 product experience | Full logistics product, ground-up, with live tracking UI |
| Performance engineering | Fan-out concurrency, semaphore tuning, p99 benchmarks |

title: OpenTrace вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Distributed Tracing System (Jaeger-Equivalent)
stack: Go В· TypeScript В· gRPC В· ClickHouse В· Kafka В· Next.js В· Kubernetes

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
OpenTrace is a complete open-source distributed tracing system built in Go and TypeScript вҖ” the portfolio centrepiece and final year project. It is architecturally equivalent to Jaeger: it receives OTLP spans over gRPC and HTTP, processes them through a Kafka pipeline with tail-based sampling, stores them in ClickHouse (10M spans/sec, p99 query < 200ms on 30-day window), and serves them via a gRPC query service to a Next.js UI with trace waterfalls, service maps, and live tail. OpenTrace instruments itself вҖ” the self-referential demo is live.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- OTLP-compatible вҖ” any OpenTelemetry SDK sends spans with zero config changes
- 10M spans/sec ingestion throughput (ClickHouse bulk insert, verified with k6)
- Tail-based sampling вҖ” keeps 100% errors + slow spans, samples 5% of normal traces
- ClickHouse columnar storage with monthly partitioning, partition pruning verified
- Self-instrumentation вҖ” OpenTrace traces its own 7 components live
- K8s Operator вҖ” all 7 components managed by a single Kubernetes Operator

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Technology | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Collector | Go + gRPC + Protobuf | OTLP/gRPC + OTLP/HTTP receiver, validation, Kafka publish |
| Pipeline Processor | Go + Kafka | Kafka consumer, tail-based sampling, ClickHouse bulk writer |
| Query Service | Go + gRPC | Trace retrieval, service map, aggregations from ClickHouse |
| API Gateway | Go + chi + REST | REST wrapper over gRPC Query Service, auth middleware |
| Live Tail Service | Go + WebSocket + Redis | Real-time span streaming, 100 concurrent tail sessions |
| Auto-Instrumentation SDK | Go + OTel | Zero-code span generation for demo microservice |
| Frontend (UI) | Next.js + D3.js + TypeScript | Trace waterfall, service map, live tail, search |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### OTLP Collector вҖ” gRPC + HTTP Receiver

The Collector implements the OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) `TraceService` gRPC service. It receives `ExportTraceServiceRequest`, validates spans (required fields, valid timestamps, trace ID format), and publishes to the `spans.raw` Kafka topic. Partition key = `trace_id` ensures all spans of a trace go to the same partition вҖ” ordering is guaranteed without sorting. Backpressure: if Kafka is slow, the Collector returns `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED`, documented in the ADR.

### Tail-Based Sampling

Tail-based sampling makes a sampling decision after the full trace arrives, not on the first span. The Pipeline Processor buffers spans per `trace_id` for 10 seconds. At decision time:
- Keep **100%** of traces with any error span
- Keep **100%** of traces with any span > 500ms
- Sample **5%** of remaining normal traces

This ensures all errors and slow traces are captured regardless of volume, while reducing storage cost for healthy traffic.

### ClickHouse Storage вҖ” Columnar at Scale

The spans table uses the MergeTree engine with `PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(start_time)`. Partition pruning is verified with `EXPLAIN`: a 24-hour query reads 1вҖ“2 partitions out of 7 total. Bulk insert batches 10,000 spans before writing вҖ” single-row vs batch benchmark shows 100x throughput difference, documented in `BENCHMARKS.md`. TTL policy: spans older than 30 days are automatically deleted. Storage cost vs Elasticsearch: **8x cheaper per GB**.

```sql
CREATE TABLE spans (
    trace_id        FixedString(32),
    span_id         FixedString(16),
    parent_span_id  FixedString(16),
    operation_name  LowCardinality(String),
    service_name    LowCardinality(String),
    start_time      DateTime64(6),
    duration_us     UInt64,
    status_code     UInt8,
    attributes      Map(String, String),
    resource        Map(String, String)
) ENGINE = MergeTree()
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(start_time)
ORDER BY (service_name, start_time, trace_id)
TTL start_time + INTERVAL 30 DAY;
```

### Self-Instrumentation Demo

OpenTrace instruments its own 7 components using the Auto-Instrumentation SDK. A live trace of a query request flows through: UI вҶ’ API Gateway вҶ’ Query Service вҶ’ ClickHouse вҖ” visible inside OpenTrace itself. The service map shows the internal dependency graph. The live tail streams spans in real time as they are generated by a demo microservice. This is the most compelling demo artifact: the system traces itself.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
- **ClickHouse spans table:** `trace_id, span_id, parent_span_id, service_name, operation_name, start_time, duration_us, status_code, tags Map(String,String), logs Array(String)`
- `PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(start_time)` вҖ” monthly partitions, TTL 30 days
- **PostgreSQL span_metadata:** `trace_id, service_name, ingested_at` вҖ” range partitioned by month
- **Redis trace cache:** `trace:{traceId}` вҶ’ serialised spans, TTL 5 min, TTL jitter Вұ30s
- **Kafka topics:** `spans.raw` (partition key: trace_id), `spans.sampled`, `spans.errors`

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Ingestion throughput | 10M spans/sec |
| ClickHouse query p99 (30-day window) | < 200ms |
| Collector gRPC p99 latency | < 5ms |
| UI waterfall render (10K spans) | < 100ms (virtualised rendering) |
| Storage cost vs Elasticsearch | 8x cheaper per GB |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` вҖ” data races in the Collector lose spans silently under load
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` вҖ” goroutine leaks in the span pipeline accumulate and crash production
- Outbox pattern for every Kafka publish вҖ” crash between receive and publish loses spans forever
- `EXPLAIN` on every ClickHouse query вҖ” partition pruning verified (Selected 1вҖ“2/7 parts)
- 10M spans/sec load test documented in `BENCHMARKS.md` вҖ” untested claims are fiction
- OTel compatibility verified вҖ” official OTel Go SDK sends to OpenTrace with zero config changes

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Infraspec Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| 0-to-1 product experience | 7-component system built ground-up, Week 1 to production |
| gRPC, webhooks, protocols | OTLP/gRPC, OTLP/HTTP, OTel SDK, Protobuf |
| Databases: SQL + NoSQL + Redis | ClickHouse + PostgreSQL + Redis вҖ” all production-tuned |
| Observability as first-class | Self-instrumented вҖ” traces its own request pipeline live |
| CI/CD, K8s, containerisation | K8s Operator manages all 7 components, GitHub Actions CI |

title: DungBeetle вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Background Job Processing Platform
stack: Go В· Kafka В· PostgreSQL В· Redis В· gRPC В· Next.js

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
DungBeetle is a production-grade background job processing platform built in Go, demonstrating leader election, exactly-once distributed cron, monolith-to-event-driven migration, and Kafka-backed job queues at scale. It begins as a Node.js monolith and is rewritten in Go вҖ” the RFC documenting the migration decision is a key deliverable. DungBeetle independently consumes job payloads from PayCore, RouteMaster, and BookWise.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Leader election via Redis SET NX вҖ” only one node runs cron at a time
- Exactly-once cron вҖ” distributed lock prevents duplicate job scheduling
- Monolith вҶ’ event-driven migration with written ADR and benchmark comparison
- Kafka-backed job queues with DLQ and exponential backoff
- Retry-with-DLQ pattern вҖ” failed jobs surfaced in UI dashboard
- AI job orchestration вҖ” LLM-powered job classification and routing

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Technology | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Job API | Go + chi + gRPC | Job submission, status queries, webhook callbacks |
| Worker Pool | Go goroutines + Kafka | Job execution, concurrency control, panic recovery |
| Scheduler | Go + Redis SETNX | Exactly-once cron, distributed leader election |
| DLQ Manager | Go + PostgreSQL | Dead-letter queue, retry policies, failure dashboard |
| Event Bus | Kafka + Outbox | Job lifecycle events, inter-service job dispatch |
| Frontend | Next.js + Shadcn UI | Job dashboard, DLQ viewer, live status feed |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Leader Election вҖ” Redis SETNX

Only one DungBeetle node should schedule cron jobs at any moment. Leader election uses `Redis SET NX EX`: the node that wins the SET becomes leader and holds the lock for 30 seconds. All nodes attempt renewal every 10 seconds. If the leader dies, the lock expires and a new leader is elected within one TTL window. Split-brain scenario tested: both nodes simultaneously try to become leader вҖ” only one wins.

### Exactly-Once Cron

A distributed lock on the cron job ID prevents duplicate scheduling across cluster nodes. When the cron fires, the scheduler atomically checks-and-sets a Redis key with the scheduled run ID. If the key already exists (another node fired first), the schedule is skipped. The result: exactly one execution per scheduled interval, even with N running nodes.

### Monolith to Event-Driven Migration

DungBeetle v0.1 is a Node.js monolith. The v1.0 Go rewrite extracts the job worker into a separate Kafka consumer. The migration RFC documents: why Go over Node.js for CPU-bound workers, goroutine-per-job model vs thread pool, `go test -race` coverage, benchmark comparison before/after. The migration demonstrates understanding of when and why to move from a monolith to event-driven.

### Retry Policy with Dead-Letter Queue

Failed jobs retry with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, up to `max_retries` (configurable per job type). After max retries, the job moves to the DLQ table with the last error payload and full retry history. The UI dashboard surfaces all DLQ entries with one-click re-queue.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
- `jobs (id, type, payload JSONB, status, priority, run_at, attempts, max_attempts, created_at)`
- `job_events (id, job_id, event_type, metadata JSONB, created_at)`
- `dlq (id, job_id, error, retry_history JSONB, moved_at)`
- `cron_schedules (id, name, cron_expr, last_run, next_run, lock_key)`
- `outbox (id, topic, payload, published_at)`

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Job throughput | 50,000 jobs/min (k6, 100 concurrent submitters) |
| Worker pick-up latency p99 | < 50ms from enqueue to execution start |
| Leader election time | < 30s (one TTL window after leader failure) |
| DLQ move latency | < 5ms after max retries exceeded |
| Cron scheduling precision | Вұ 1s of scheduled time |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes вҖ” concurrent job dispatch must be safe
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` вҖ” goroutine leaks in the worker pool accumulate over days
- Leader election split-brain scenario tested and verified
- Cron exactly-once semantics verified: N nodes, single execution per interval
- DLQ: crash between enqueue and Kafka publish tested, recovery verified
- Migration RFC written: Node.js monolith вҶ’ Go event-driven, with benchmark numbers

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Infraspec Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Event-driven architecture | Kafka-backed queues, outbox pattern, Saga choreography |
| Distributed systems depth | Leader election, distributed cron, fencing tokens |
| Monolith vs microservices | Written ADR with benchmark comparison |
| AI tools for development | AI job classification and routing built in |
| RFCs and decision documents | Migration RFC + every architecture decision documented |

title: BookWise вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Distributed Seat Reservation System
stack: Go В· PostgreSQL В· Redis В· Kafka В· gRPC В· Next.js

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
BookWise is a distributed seat reservation system built in Go, demonstrating the classical concurrency and correctness problems of booking systems at scale: distributed locking with fencing tokens, double-booking prevention under 10,000 concurrent users, payment Saga choreography, and a waitlist state machine. Every reservation is guaranteed to be mutually exclusive. Every payment failure releases the held seat.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Distributed locks with fencing tokens вҖ” prevents double-booking under high concurrency
- Double-booking prevention verified at 10,000 concurrent users (k6)
- Payment Saga вҖ” reserve seat вҶ’ charge payment вҶ’ confirm booking with compensation
- Waitlist state machine вҖ” automatic promotion when a cancellation occurs
- `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` вҖ” correct row-level locking for seat holds
- Isolation level demos вҖ” dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read triggered live in psql

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Technology | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Booking Service | Go + PostgreSQL | Seat availability, reservation creation, hold management |
| Lock Service | Go + Redis | Distributed locks, fencing tokens, TTL-based expiry |
| Payment Gateway | Go + Kafka + Saga | Payment processing, Saga orchestration, compensation |
| Waitlist Engine | Go + PostgreSQL | Queue management, automatic promotion, notifications |
| Query Service | Go + gRPC | Read-optimised availability queries, Redis cache |
| Frontend | Next.js + Tanstack Query | Seat map, booking flow, waitlist position tracker |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Distributed Locking with Fencing Tokens

A seat hold acquires a Redis distributed lock with a monotonically increasing fencing token. The token is included in every subsequent database write. If an expired lock holder attempts a late write, the database rejects it (`token < current_token`). This eliminates the race condition where a slow process holds an expired lock and overwrites a newer reservation. Correctness test: 10,000 goroutines simultaneously attempt to book the same seat вҖ” exactly one succeeds.

### SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

Seat availability queries use `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` to atomically claim a seat row without blocking other workers on already-locked rows. This is the correct pattern for a reservation system: a worker picks only rows it can immediately lock, processes them, and releases. Workers never block each other. The pattern is documented in an ADR comparing it to optimistic locking.

### Payment Saga вҖ” Seat Reservation Flow

The booking flow is a three-step Saga:
1. **Hold seat** вҖ” acquire distributed lock, mark seat as `HELD`
2. **Charge payment** вҖ” call payment gateway via Kafka event
3. **Confirm booking** вҖ” update seat to `CONFIRMED`, release lock

On payment failure, a compensating event releases the hold and returns the seat to `AVAILABLE`. If the process crashes between steps, the hold TTL (120s) expires and the seat is automatically released.

### Waitlist State Machine

When a booking is cancelled, the Waitlist Engine is notified via a Kafka event. The engine promotes the first eligible waitlist entry: notifies the user, initiates a payment Saga, and starts a 15-minute confirmation window. If the promoted user fails to confirm, the next waitlist entry is promoted.

State transitions: `WAITING вҶ’ OFFERED вҶ’ CONFIRMED | EXPIRED вҶ’ WAITING (next user)`

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
- `seats (id, event_id, section, row, number, status, held_until, fencing_token)`
- `reservations (id, seat_id, user_id, status, payment_id, idempotency_key, created_at)`
- `waitlist (id, event_id, user_id, position, status, offered_at, expires_at)`
- `payment_events (id, reservation_id, event_type, payload JSONB, created_at)`
- `outbox (id, topic, payload, published_at)`

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Concurrent booking test | 10,000 goroutines, 1 seat вҖ” exactly 1 succeeds (k6) |
| Seat hold acquisition p99 | < 10ms (Redis SETNX + fencing token) |
| Booking throughput | 3,000 reservations/min under load |
| Waitlist promotion latency | < 500ms from cancellation to notification |
| Payment Saga p99 | < 2s end-to-end (hold вҶ’ charge вҶ’ confirm) |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes вҖ” concurrent seat access is the core correctness problem
- Fencing token correctness test: 10K concurrent attempts, exactly 1 succeeds
- Saga compensation: payment failure triggers seat release, verified
- Waitlist promotion: cancellation triggers automatic promotion, verified
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on every query вҖ” zero seq scans on seats/reservations tables
- Isolation level anomaly demos вҖ” phantom read triggered live in psql

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Infraspec Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Distributed systems depth | Fencing-token distributed locks, exactly-once reservation |
| 0-to-1 product experience | Full booking product built ground-up, Week 1 to production |
| SQL and PostgreSQL depth | SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, isolation levels, partitioning |
| Event-driven architecture | Saga choreography, Kafka outbox, waitlist state machine |
| Debugging + production issues | Concurrent lock contention, phantom read demo, PITR drill |

title: WAL-Backed KV Store вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Write-Ahead Log from Scratch

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
WAL design, crash recovery, and log compaction вҖ” the exact internals PostgreSQL, SQLite, and RocksDB use for durability. Most engineers use databases that depend on a WAL without ever understanding why. After this project, you can explain in an interview exactly what happens between a `COMMIT` statement and the data being safe on disk.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Durability before acknowledgement вҖ” `fsync()` called before returning success.
- Idempotent recovery вҖ” replaying the same WAL twice produces the same result.
- Checksum for partial write detection вҖ” catches interruptions mid-write.
- Snapshot + WAL hybrid вҖ” combines durability with bounded log growth.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| WAL Writer | Append-only log with checksums. Every write durably recorded before in-memory update. |
| Recovery Engine | Replay WAL from last good checkpoint on startup. Store returns to exact pre-crash state. |
| Log Compaction | Snapshot current in-memory state to new file, delete all WAL entries before the snapshot. |
| HTTP API | `GET /key`, `PUT /key`, `DELETE /key` вҖ” same interface as the original in-memory store. |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### WAL Entry Format
```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  op (1 byte) в”Ӯ key_len (4 bytes) в”Ӯ val_len (4 bytes) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  key (n bytes) в”Ӯ value (m bytes)                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  timestamp (8 bytes) в”Ӯ checksum (4 bytes CRC32)      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```
- **op:** `0x01` = SET, `0x02` = DEL
- **checksum:** CRC32 over all preceding bytes in the entry. If checksum doesn't match on read вҶ’ partial write вҶ’ stop replay.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Recovery Algorithm
1. Load latest snapshot file (if exists) вҶ’ populate in-memory map
2. Open WAL file at snapshot LSN (Log Sequence Number)
3. For each entry: verify checksum вҶ’ apply to map вҶ’ advance LSN
4. Stop at first checksum failure (partial write boundary)

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Write throughput (fsync on) | Baseline durability |
| Write throughput (fsync off) | 10вҖ“100x faster tradeoff |
| Recovery time (1M entries) | < 5 seconds |
| Compaction time (1M entries) | < 2 seconds |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- `WALWriter` must call `fsync()` before success
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` clean
- Crash simulation: verify recovery correctness after SIGKILL

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Database Internals | Implementation of WAL/Checkpoints used in PG/SQLite |
| Performance Engineering | Benchmarking fsync overhead vs durability |
| Systems Design | Durability guarantees in distributed KV stores |

title: TCP Connection Pool вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: PgBouncer from Scratch (pgwire Protocol)

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Why connection pools exist and how they work at the TCP level. Most engineers configure PgBouncer without understanding its multiplexing logic. After this project, you understand how 10,000 app connections can live on 9 database connections.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- pgwire protocol parsing вҖ” PostgreSQL's binary wire protocol internals.
- Transaction mode vs session mode вҖ” 100x efficiency for HTTP APIs.
- Backpressure вҖ” blocking client queries when backend connections are busy.
- Connection lifecycle вҖ” healthy pings and removal of dead idle connections.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| TCP Listener | Accepts client connections on local port |
| pgwire Parser | Reads PG wire protocol frames (Startup, Query, Response) |
| Connection Pool | Maintains real connections to PG backend |
| Session Router | In transaction mode: assigns connection for duration of one TX |
| Health Checker | Periodically pings idle backend connections |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Transaction Mode Logic
On client `Query("BEGIN")`:
1. Acquire backend connection from pool (block if none)
2. Assign connection to this client session
3. Forward BEGIN to PostgreSQL

On `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`:
1. Forward to PostgreSQL
2. Release backend connection back to pool

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Pool Sizing Formula
`pool_size = (num_CPUs Г— 2) + effective_spindle_count` 

For 4-CPU DB:
`(4 Г— 2) + 1 = 9` connections.

Result: 10,000 app connections вҶ’ 9 PostgreSQL connections.

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Acquisition p99 | < 1ms |
| 1000 concurrent clients | Stable without errors |
| Memory overhead | ~11x reduction in DB process count |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- Transaction boundary detection token scan correct
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` clean
- Health checker must purge dead connections within 2 TTL windows

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Networking | Raw TCP + pgwire protocol implementation |
| Performance | Connection multiplexing at scale |
| DB Internals | PgBouncer-equivalent transaction architecture |

title: Clustered WebSocket Server вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Real-Time Chat with Redis Pub/Sub Fan-Out

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Scaling WebSocket servers horizontally using Redis pub/sub as the message bus. A message sent to Node A is delivered to a client on Node B вҖ” a fundamental distributed systems problem appearing in Slack, Uber, and RouteMaster.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Redis pub/sub fan-out вҖ” O(1) calls reach all nodes.
- Ping/pong keepalive вҖ” prevents dead connection registry accumulation.
- sync.Map registry вҖ” thread-safe management without global locks.
- Distributed presence вҖ” tracking user-node mapping in Redis.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| WS Server | Accepts upgrades and manages connection registry |
| Registry | `sync.Map` of `userID вҶ’ *websocket.Conn` |
| Publisher | `PUBLISH channel:roomID message` to Redis |
| Subscriber | Receive messages from other nodes, fan out locally |
| Presence | HSET with TTL in Redis to track live nodes |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Distributed Presence Pattern
On connect: `HSet "presence:" + userID, "nodeID", os.Getenv("NODE_ID")`. 
TTL: 60s. 
Heartbeat: renew every 30s. 
On disconnect: delete key.

Presence enables finding which node a user is on, while pub/sub handles the actual delivery regardless of node.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Message Flow
1. Node 1 receives message for UserB.
2. Not local? вҶ’ `PUBLISH` to Redis.
3. All nodes receive `PUBLISH`.
4. Node 2 has UserB local? вҶ’ deliver via WS.
5. Node 3 doesn't? вҶ’ discard.

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Cross-node p99 | < 10ms |
| Per-node capacity | 5,000 connections |
| Memory / connection | < 10KB |
| Detection time | < 60s |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes everywhere
- Ping/Pong handler MUST close on failure
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` for subscriber goroutines
- Redis channels must be unique per room/user

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Distributed Systems | Pub/sub fan-out + stateful server clustering |
| Real-time | WebSocket protocol + low-latency delivery |
| Systems Design | Slack/Uber-style messaging architecture |

title: Recursive DNS Resolver вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Full DNS Resolution from Root Servers

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
How DNS actually works at the wire level вҖ” not just theory, but full recursive resolution path from root servers down to authoritative ones. Essential for understanding every backend request starting point.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- DNS over UDP вҖ” parsing binary wire format (< 512 bytes).
- Recursive vs Iterative вҖ” implementing the loop from root hints.
- TTL Caching вҖ” making DNS scalable by respecting response TTLs.
- Label Compression вҖ” handling binary pointers in the wire format.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| UDP Client | Port 53 queries with 2s timeout/retries |
| Parser/Builder | Binary DNS header/question/record logic |
| Resolver | Iterative loop: Root вҶ’ TLD вҶ’ Authoritative |
| Cache | `sync.RWMutex` backed TTL-aware map |
| Root Hints | Hardcoded IPs of 13 root clusters |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### label compression example
In binary, `\xc0\x0c` means "jump to offset 12". This saves space when the same domain appears multiple times (Question vs Answer sections). Implementing this correctly is mandatory for parsing real-world responses from Google/Cloudflare.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Supported types
- **A (1)**: IPv4 address
- **NS (2)**: Nameserver hostname
- **CNAME (5)**: Alias resolution
- **MX (15)**: Mail server priorities

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Cold | Warm |
|---|---|---|
| Simple A | 100-500ms | < 1ms |
| CNAME (3 hops) | 300-900ms | < 1ms |
| Cache Hit Rate | вҖ” | > 90% |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- Compare output exactly with `dig +trace` results
- UDP timeout/retries must handle packet loss
- `sync.RWMutex` used for concurrent cache access

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Networking | Raw UDP + Binary protocol parsing |
| Systems Knowledge | Root server hierarchy + TLD resolution |
| Performance | TTL caching + CNAME chain optimization |

title: Notification Delivery Service вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Multi-Channel Reliable Delivery with Idempotency

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Reliable multi-channel notification system (Email, SMS, Push, Webhook). Guarantees at-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers. Standalone service consumed by RouteMaster and PayCore.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Outbox Pattern вҖ” Atomic persistence before dispatch.
- Idempotency вҖ” No duplicate emails/SMS on retry.
- Exponential Backoff вҖ” Jittered retries for provider outages.
- HMAC Webhooks вҖ” Signed delivery for security.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| REST API | Accepts requests with idempotency key |
| Outbox | Persists to PG before delivery attempt |
| Dispatcher | Routes to Email/SMS/Push/Webhook handlers |
| Retry Worker | Exponential backoff logic + DLQ management |
| Idempotency | Redis key-result store (24h TTL) |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### Exponential Backoff with Jitter
Formula: `min(base Г— 2^attempt + jitter, max)`. 
Jitter prevents "thundering herd" where 10,000 retries fire at the exact same millisecond after a 3rd party service (like Twilio) recovers. This is a critical production resiliency pattern.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Delivery State Machine
`PENDING вҶ’ DISPATCHED вҶ’ DELIVERED` 
or 
`FAILED вҶ’ RETRYING вҶ’ DELIVERED | DLQ` 

Transaction integrity: writing notification + business event in same PG transaction via Outbox.

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Ingestion throughput | 10,000/min |
| Webhook Latency | < 500ms |
| Redis Lookup | < 1ms |
| Success Rate | > 99% after recovery |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- `hmac.Equal` used for timing-safe signature check
- Outbox must be cleaned up periodically
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` for retry workers

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Reliability | Outbox pattern + Idempotency guarantees |
| Architecture | Channel abstraction + Event-driven retry loop |
| Resiliency | Exponential backoff + Jitter implementation |

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Email protocol internals: SMTP command sequence, MX routing, STARTTLS security, and MIME parsing. Understand what happens at the TCP level when you send/receive an email.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- SMTP State Machine вҖ” EHLO/MAIL/RCPT/DATA/QUIT loop.
- STARTTLS вҖ” In-place plaintext-to-TLS upgrade.
- MIME Parser вҖ” Extracting headers, body, and attachments.
- MX Routing вҖ” DNS-based server discovery for outbound mail.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| SMTP Machine | Command sequence (250/354/221 codes) |
| TLS Upgrade | STARTTLS handler for transport security |
| MX Resolver | `net.LookupMX` for routing outbound mail |
| Parser | MIME multi-part / boundary handling |
| Storage | Persistent storage in PostgreSQL |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### STARTTLS In-Place Upgrade
Unlike HTTPS which uses port 443, SMTP starts on port 25 as plaintext. The `STARTTLS` command initiates a handshake on the *same connection*. The Go server must wrap the existing `net.Conn` with `tls.Server` without closing the socket. This is a unique protocol pattern compared to most modern systems.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### MX Priority Routing
To send to `user@gmail.com`:
1. Lookup MX records.
2. Sort by priority (lowest first).
3. Try connect to highest priority.
4. Failover to next on error.

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Ingestion | 1,000/min |
| SMTP Handshake | < 100ms |
| TLS Overhead | < 10ms |
| Message Parse | < 5ms |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- SMTP status codes (2xx, 4xx, 5xx) used correctly
- Self-signed TLS certs for development
- `net.LookupMX` used for all outbound routing

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Protocols | Implementing RFC 5321 (SMTP) from scratch |
| Networking | MX routing + TCP stream state machines |
| Security | STARTTLS + SPF/DKIM concept depth |

title: Distributed Lock Service вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: Leader Election + Fencing Tokens

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Implementing correct distributed locks with fencing-tokens to avoid corruption during GC pauses/network partitions. Essential for leader election in DungBeetle and seat locks in BookWise.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- Fencing Tokens вҖ” Monotonic counters to reject stale writes.
- SET NX EX вҖ” Atomic set-if-not-exists foundations.
- Lua Atomicity вҖ” Conditional GET/SET/DEL in Redis.
- Split-brain Testing вҖ” Simulating network partitions.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| gRPC API | Acquire/Release/Renew contract |
| Token Gen | `INCR` in Redis for monotonic values |
| Lock Store | Redis `SET NX EX` with token values |
| Verifier | DB update logic checking `token >= current` |
| Heartbeat | TTL/3 renewal loop to maintain ownership |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### The Fencing Token Problem
TTL alone is unsafe. If Process A pauses (GC/Disk), its lock expires. Process B acquires it. Process A resumes and writes. CORRUPTION. 
Solution: Every lock has a token (e.g. 101). Process B gets 102. DB rejects Process A's write because `101 < 102`. This is the only way to build safe distributed locks.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Lua Atomic Release
```lua
local current = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])
if current == ARGV[1] then
    redis.call('DEL', KEYS[1])
    return 1
else
    return 0
end
```
Prevents a process from accidentally releasing a lock it no longer owns (someone else took it after expiry).

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Acquire p99 | < 5ms |
| Throughput | 10,000/sec |
| Resign on fail | < 30s (TTL) |
| Split-brain resolution | < 1 TTL window |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- Monitonic token increment always used
- Lua script used for all Acquire/Release ops
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` clean

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Distributed Systems | Fencing tokens + mutual exclusion logic |
| Consistency | Correctness proof against GC pauses/partitions |
| Systems Design | Robust leader election pattern implementation |

title: LSM-Tree Storage Engine вҖ” Project Specification
subtitle: RocksDB / LevelDB Write Path from Scratch

sections.title: 1. Project Overview
sections.content:
Log-Structured Merge-Tree (LSM-tree) architecture used by RocksDB/Cassandra/ClickHouse. Optimized for write-heavy workloads by converting random disk I/O into sequential writes. Understand the tradeoff: fast writes vs read/write amplification.

---

sections.title: 2. Key Demonstrations
sections.content:
- MemTable flushes вҖ” Sequential disk flushes of sorted maps.
- Bloom Filters вҖ” Probabilistic skipping of irrelevent SSTables.
- Size-Tiered Compaction вҖ” Background merging to reduce amplification.
- Tombstones вҖ” Mark-for-delete semantics for immutable storage.

---

sections.title: 3. Architecture
sections.content:
| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| MemTable | B-tree/Red-black tree in-memory sorted map |
| SSTable | Immutable sorted file format on disk |
| Bloom Filter | Bitset for O(1) membership check |
| Compaction | K-way merge of L0 SSTables into L1 |
| WAL | Durability guarantee for MemTable writes |

---

sections.title: 4. Technical Depth
sections.content:
### SSTable File Format
- **Data Block**: Sorted KV pairs.
- **Index Block**: Sparse index (every 16th key) for binary search.
- **Bloom Filter**: Bit array to avoid disk RTT on misses.
- **Footer**: Offsets for index and filter blocks.
Binary search on Index Block вҶ’ Locate Data Block вҶ’ Scan block. Bloom filter skips this entire process 99% of time on misses.

---

sections.title: 5. Data Model
sections.content:
### Bloom Filter Math
For 1% false positive rate: ~10 bits per element and 7 hash functions.
Formula: `m = -n * ln(p) / (ln(2))ВІ` 
LSM-trees depend on this math to maintain read performance as file counts grow.

---

sections.title: 6. Benchmarks
sections.content:
| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Write throughput | > 500K/sec |
| Read latency (Bloom) | < 2ms |
| Recovery (1M keys) | < 5s |
| Compaction | > 100MB/sec |

---

sections.title: 7. Non-Negotiable Rules
sections.content:
- `go test -race ./...` passes
- Tombstones must be purged during L1+ compaction
- Bloom filter false positive rate < 1% verified
- `goleak.VerifyNone(t)` for background workers

---

sections.title: 8. Infraspec Requirement Mapping
sections.content:
| Requirement | How This Project Delivers It |
|---|---|
| Storage Engines | Raw implementation of LSM-tree write path |
| Performance | Sequential vs Random disk I/O optimization |
| Algorithms | K-way merge + Bloom filters + Sparse indexes |
